items.myth: cProfile is for advanced performance work only.
items.reality: It's a first-resort tool. The 30-second `python -m cProfile -s cumulative script.py` is often the most productive thing you can do when something feels slow.
items.example: Spent 20 minutes guessing? Stop. Run cProfile, read the top 10 lines, then guess less.

items.myth: High call count is automatically bad.
items.reality: It depends. A simple comparator called a million times in a tight `sorted(...)` is normal; a `re.compile(...)` called a million times is a bug. The metric to watch is **time spent**, not raw call count.
items.example: Calling `len()` 1M times is fine; calling `json.loads()` 1M times almost certainly isn't.

items.myth: cProfile slows code so much its numbers are meaningless.
items.reality: Absolute times are inflated; *ratios* are reliable. cProfile is for finding the relative bottleneck, not microbenchmarking exact wall times.
items.example: If cProfile says A takes 60% and B 40%, that ratio holds in production even though both are faster there.

items.myth: If cProfile shows a numpy call as the hot spot, you can drill into it.
items.reality: cProfile only sees Python function boundaries. Inside numpy (or any C extension) everything is opaque; the call appears as one entry. Use `line_profiler` for pure-Python lines, dedicated tools for C-level analysis.
items.example: `np.einsum(...)` shows up as a single hot line — drill-down isn't possible from Python.

items.myth: Once cProfile says X is slow, the fix is obvious.
items.reality: Profiling tells you *where* — figuring out *how* to make it faster (algorithm change, batching, caching, C extension) is the actual engineering work. Don't conflate diagnosis with treatment.
items.example: `re.compile` per call is the diagnosis; module-level compiled regex is the treatment.
